pillar.metaDescription: Find caregiver support groups near you, spot burnout early, and learn how to get paid as a family caregiver. Free senior help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.h1: Support Groups for Caregivers Near Me
pillar.quickAnswer: Find caregiver support groups near you through your local Area Agency on Aging (call 800-677-1116), the Family Caregiver Alliance, and Alzheimer's Association chapters (800-272-3900). Most are free, meet in-person or online, and serve over 53 million U.S. family caregivers, per AARP's 2020 Caregiving report.
pillar.heroSubhead: Free, confidential help finding support groups, respite, and caregiver pay programs — one call to 1-800-MEDIGAP connects you.
pillar.intro: Caring for an aging parent or spouse is one of the hardest jobs in America — and you should not do it alone. This guide shows you where to find caregiver support groups near you and how to protect your own health while you help someone else.

sections.h2: Where can I find caregiver support groups near me?
sections.body: Start with three reliable, no-cost sources. First, your local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) maintains lists of in-person and virtual groups — reach them through the federal Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 or eldercare.acl.gov. Second, the Family Caregiver Alliance and the Caregiver Action Network run national online forums and connect you to regional groups. Third, disease-specific organizations like the Alzheimer's Association (800-272-3900) host dementia caregiver groups in most communities. Hospitals, faith communities, and senior centers also host meetings. Online options such as Smart Patients and AARP's caregiving community help when you cannot leave home. If you need help navigating these, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will point you to the right resource.

sections.h2: Why do caregiver support groups matter?
sections.body: Caregiving is rewarding but isolating. AARP and the National Alliance for Caregiving estimate that 53 million Americans provide unpaid care, and roughly 1 in 5 report their own health has gotten worse because of it. Support groups reduce that isolation by connecting you with people who understand exactly what you face — managing medications, handling difficult behaviors, and grieving the slow loss of a parent you love. Members share practical tips (how to get respite, which benefits to apply for) and emotional relief. Studies from the Family Caregiver Alliance link peer support to lower caregiver depression and delayed nursing-home placement for the person receiving care.

sections.h2: What free caregiver resources exist beyond support groups?
sections.body: Support groups are one piece. The National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP), funded under the Older Americans Act, provides counseling, training, and respite care through your AAA — no income test. Medicaid programs in most states pay family caregivers through self-directed care or HCBS waivers. The VA offers a stipend and health coverage to eligible family caregivers of veterans. The Eldercare Locator (800-677-1116) and 2-1-1 connect you to meal delivery, transportation, and adult day programs. Many caregivers also qualify for tax credits. To understand which Medicare, Medicaid, and benefit programs fit your family, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, no-pressure guidance.

sections.h2: How do I recognize caregiver burnout before it harms me?
sections.body: Watch for warning signs: constant exhaustion that sleep does not fix, withdrawing from friends, irritability or resentment toward the person you care for, frequent illness, changes in appetite, and feeling hopeless. The Mayo Clinic notes that untreated caregiver stress raises your risk of depression, anxiety, and chronic disease. The fix is not to try harder — it is to share the load. Use respite care to take real breaks, accept help from family, join a support group, and protect your own medical appointments. If you feel you might harm yourself or the person you care for, call or text 988 (the Suicide and Crisis Lifeline) immediately.

sections.h2: Can I get paid to care for my aging parent?
sections.body: Often, yes. Original Medicare generally does not pay family caregivers for personal care, but several other programs do. Most state Medicaid programs let an eligible senior hire a family member through self-directed or 'consumer-directed' care. The VA's Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers pays a monthly stipend to qualifying caregivers of eligible veterans. Some long-term care insurance policies and state-funded programs also reimburse family caregivers. Eligibility depends on the senior's income, the state, and the level of care needed. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you figure out which paid-caregiver path your family may qualify for.

faqs.q: Are caregiver support groups free?
faqs.a: Yes, most caregiver support groups are free. Groups run by Area Agencies on Aging, the Alzheimer's Association, the Family Caregiver Alliance, hospitals, and faith communities charge nothing to attend. The federally funded National Family Caregiver Support Program also offers free counseling and respite with no income requirement.

faqs.q: How do I find a caregiver support group near me?
faqs.a: Call the Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 or visit eldercare.acl.gov to reach your local Area Agency on Aging, which lists nearby groups. For dementia, call the Alzheimer's Association at 800-272-3900. Hospitals, senior centers, and online communities like AARP's also host groups.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay family caregivers?
faqs.a: Original Medicare generally does not pay family members for personal or custodial care. However, Medicaid (in most states), the VA caregiver stipend, and some Medicare Advantage supplemental benefits may provide caregiver pay or support. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn which programs your family may qualify for.

faqs.q: What are the first signs of caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: Early signs include exhaustion that rest does not relieve, withdrawing from friends and activities, irritability or resentment, trouble sleeping, getting sick more often, and feeling hopeless or overwhelmed. Recognizing these signs early lets you add respite, support groups, and help before burnout damages your health.

faqs.q: What is the National Family Caregiver Support Program?
faqs.a: The National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP) is a federal program under the Older Americans Act that funds counseling, training, support groups, and respite care for family caregivers. It is delivered through local Area Agencies on Aging, has no income test, and is free to eligible caregivers.

faqs.q: Can I get respite care so I can take a break?
faqs.a: Yes. Respite care gives caregivers temporary relief through in-home aides, adult day programs, or short-term facility stays. Funding may come from the National Family Caregiver Support Program, Medicaid waivers, the VA, or private pay. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 to arrange it.

faqs.q: Where can I get help in a caregiving emergency or mental health crisis?
faqs.a: If you or the person you care for is in immediate danger, call 911. For emotional crisis or thoughts of self-harm, call or text the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline, available free 24/7. For urgent non-medical caregiving help, dial 2-1-1 to reach local social services.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help caregivers?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for all things senior in America. We help caregivers find support groups, respite, and benefit programs, and we explain how Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid fit your family's situation — free and with no pressure. Call 1-800-633-4427.

children.keyword: support groups for caregivers near me

page.metaDescription: Find free caregiver support groups near you — in-person and online. Get connected fast. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free senior caregiving help.
page.h1: Support Groups for Caregivers Near Me
page.quickAnswer: Find caregiver support groups near you by calling the Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 to reach your Area Agency on Aging, or the Alzheimer's Association at 800-272-3900 for dementia groups. Most groups are free and meet in person or online, serving America's 53 million family caregivers, per AARP.
page.heroSubhead: Free, confidential help connecting you to caregiver support groups near you — one call to 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: You do not have to carry caregiving alone. Here is exactly where to find a support group near you, in person or online, usually at no cost.

sections.h2: How do I find a caregiver support group near me?
sections.body: Use three trusted starting points. Call the Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 (or visit eldercare.acl.gov) to reach your local Area Agency on Aging, which keeps current lists of nearby groups. For dementia or Alzheimer's caregiving, call the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 helpline at 800-272-3900. For online groups you can join from home, try the Family Caregiver Alliance, Caregiver Action Network, and AARP's caregiving community. Hospitals, senior centers, and faith communities frequently host meetings too. If you are unsure which fits, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you find the right group.

sections.h2: In-person vs. online caregiver support groups
sections.body: In-person groups offer face-to-face connection and are often hosted by hospitals, senior centers, or disease organizations near you. Online groups — forums, video meetings, and apps — let you participate at any hour without leaving the person you care for, which matters when respite is hard to arrange. Many caregivers use both. Disease-specific groups (dementia, Parkinson's, cancer, stroke) connect you with people facing the same challenges, while general caregiver groups cover shared issues like burnout, benefits, and family stress.

sections.h2: Are caregiver support groups confidential and free?
sections.body: Almost all caregiver support groups are free, and reputable groups keep discussions confidential so you can speak openly. Groups funded through the National Family Caregiver Support Program, the Alzheimer's Association, and Area Agencies on Aging charge nothing and require no income test. You will never be asked to buy anything to attend. If a group ever pressures you to purchase a product, treat that as a red flag and look elsewhere.

faqs.q: How do I find a caregiver support group near me?
faqs.a: Call the Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 to reach your local Area Agency on Aging, which lists nearby groups. For dementia, call the Alzheimer's Association at 800-272-3900. Hospitals, senior centers, and online communities also host free groups.

faqs.q: Are caregiver support groups free?
faqs.a: Yes. Most caregiver support groups, including those run by Area Agencies on Aging, the Alzheimer's Association, and the Family Caregiver Alliance, are completely free to attend and require no income qualification.

faqs.q: Can I join a caregiver support group online?
faqs.a: Yes. Organizations like the Family Caregiver Alliance, Caregiver Action Network, and AARP offer online forums and video support groups you can join from home, which is ideal when you cannot leave the person you care for.

faqs.q: Is there a support group specifically for dementia caregivers?
faqs.a: Yes. The Alzheimer's Association runs dementia caregiver support groups in most communities and a free 24/7 helpline at 800-272-3900. Your Area Agency on Aging can also point you to local dementia-specific groups.

faqs.q: Who can help me find the right caregiver resources?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, can help you find support groups, respite, and benefit programs, plus explain how Medicare and Medicaid fit your family. Call 1-800-633-4427 for free.

page.metaDescription: Know the warning signs of caregiver burnout — exhaustion, resentment, illness — and how to get help. Free senior caregiving support: call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Caregiver burnout symptoms include constant exhaustion, withdrawing from others, irritability or resentment, trouble sleeping, frequent illness, appetite changes, and feeling hopeless. The Mayo Clinic warns untreated caregiver stress raises the risk of depression and chronic disease. Respite care and support groups help reverse it.
page.heroSubhead: Recognize burnout early and get free help — one call to 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Caregiver burnout creeps up slowly. Knowing the symptoms early lets you get relief before it harms your health or your ability to care for someone you love.

sections.h2: What are the symptoms of caregiver burnout?
sections.body: Caregiver burnout is physical, emotional, and mental exhaustion from prolonged caregiving stress. Common symptoms include: exhaustion that rest does not fix; withdrawing from friends, family, and activities you enjoyed; irritability, anger, or resentment toward the person you care for; trouble sleeping or sleeping too much; getting sick more often; changes in appetite or weight; difficulty concentrating; and feelings of hopelessness or that your own life no longer matters. The Mayo Clinic reports that prolonged caregiver stress increases the risk of depression, anxiety, and chronic illness. Burnout is not a personal failure — it is a signal that you need more support.

sections.h2: What causes caregiver burnout?
sections.body: Burnout builds when caregiving demands outpace your resources and rest. Common drivers include doing too much alone, unclear or unrealistic expectations, financial strain, lack of privacy or time off, role confusion (acting as both child and caregiver to a parent), and the emotional weight of watching a loved one decline. Caregivers of people with dementia face especially high burnout rates because of the behavioral and round-the-clock demands. The fix is rarely 'try harder' — it is sharing the load through respite, family help, and outside support.

sections.h2: When should I get help for burnout?
sections.body: Get help as soon as you notice the symptoms above — do not wait until you collapse. Talk to your own doctor about your stress, arrange respite care through your Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116), and join a caregiver support group. If you ever have thoughts of harming yourself or the person you care for, this is an emergency: call or text 988, the Suicide and Crisis Lifeline, available free 24/7. To find respite and benefit programs that lighten your load, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What are the main symptoms of caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: Key symptoms are exhaustion that rest does not relieve, withdrawing from others, irritability or resentment, sleep changes, getting sick often, appetite changes, trouble concentrating, and feeling hopeless. Several symptoms together signal it is time to add support and respite.

faqs.q: Is caregiver burnout the same as depression?
faqs.a: They overlap but are not identical. Burnout is exhaustion from caregiving stress, while depression is a clinical mood disorder. Untreated burnout can lead to depression, so if low mood, hopelessness, or loss of interest persist, talk to your doctor.

faqs.q: How do I recover from caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: Recovery starts with sharing the load: arrange respite care, accept help from family, join a support group, protect your own medical appointments, and set realistic limits. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for respite options.

faqs.q: Can caregiver burnout affect my physical health?
faqs.a: Yes. The Mayo Clinic notes that prolonged caregiver stress raises the risk of high blood pressure, weakened immunity, depression, anxiety, and chronic disease. Protecting your own health is essential to keep caring for someone else.

faqs.q: Where can I get help if burnout feels like a crisis?
faqs.a: If you have thoughts of harming yourself or the person you care for, call or text 988, the free 24/7 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. For urgent caregiving support, dial 2-1-1, or call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find respite and relief programs.

page.metaDescription: Does Medicare pay family caregivers? Mostly no — but Medicaid, the VA, and other programs may. Find out what you qualify for. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Original Medicare generally does not pay family members for personal or custodial caregiving. However, Medicaid (in most states), the VA caregiver stipend, certain Medicare Advantage supplemental benefits, and state programs may provide caregiver pay. Eligibility depends on the senior's income, state, and care needs.
page.heroSubhead: Find out which programs may pay you to care for a loved one — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Many caregivers ask whether Medicare will pay them for the care they already give. Here is the honest answer, plus the programs that actually do pay family caregivers.

sections.h2: Does Medicare pay family caregivers?
sections.body: In most cases, no. Original Medicare (Parts A and B) does not pay family members for personal care, household help, or supervision — services it classifies as custodial care. Medicare does cover skilled care, such as part-time home health from a Medicare-certified agency when a doctor orders it, but that pays the agency's licensed staff, not a family member. So if you are providing day-to-day help with bathing, meals, or medications, Original Medicare will not write you a check. The good news: other programs may. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you sort out which ones fit your family.

sections.h2: What programs DO pay family caregivers?
sections.body: Several pathways exist. Medicaid is the biggest: most states let an eligible senior hire a family member through self-directed or consumer-directed care programs and Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers. The VA's Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers pays a monthly stipend to qualifying caregivers of eligible veterans. Some Medicare Advantage plans now offer supplemental in-home support benefits, though these rarely pay relatives directly. Long-term care insurance policies sometimes reimburse family caregivers, and a few states fund their own caregiver-pay programs. Each has its own rules and income limits.

sections.h2: How do I find out what I qualify for?
sections.body: Start by identifying the senior's coverage (Medicaid, VA, Medicare Advantage) and state of residence, since rules vary widely. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116, your state Medicaid office, or the VA for caregiver programs. Because the rules are complex and change often, talking to a knowledgeable, licensed resource saves time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free, no-pressure help understanding how Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid interact and which caregiver-pay programs your family may be eligible for.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay family members to be caregivers?
faqs.a: Generally no. Original Medicare does not pay family members for personal or custodial care. It covers doctor-ordered skilled home health through certified agencies, which pays the agency's staff, not relatives. Medicaid and the VA are more likely to pay family caregivers.

faqs.q: Will Medicare Advantage pay my caregiver costs?
faqs.a: Some Medicare Advantage plans offer supplemental in-home support or respite benefits, but they rarely pay family members directly. Benefits vary by plan and location. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review what a specific plan includes for caregiver support.

faqs.q: How can I actually get paid to care for a parent?
faqs.a: The most common routes are Medicaid self-directed care programs, the VA caregiver stipend for eligible veterans' families, and some long-term care insurance policies. Eligibility depends on the senior's income, state, and care needs. We can help you check.

faqs.q: Does Medigap cover caregiver pay?
faqs.a: No. Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policies help pay Medicare's out-of-pocket costs like deductibles and coinsurance. They do not pay family caregivers. For caregiver pay, look to Medicaid, the VA, or state programs.

faqs.q: Who can explain my caregiver-pay options?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free guidance on how Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid work and which caregiver-pay programs your family may qualify for. Call 1-800-633-4427.

page.metaDescription: The VA caregiver stipend pays eligible family caregivers of veterans monthly. Learn who qualifies and how to apply. Free senior help: call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The VA caregiver stipend, part of the Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC), pays a monthly stipend to eligible family caregivers of seriously injured or ill veterans. Qualifying caregivers may also receive health coverage, training, and respite. Apply through VA.gov or call 855-260-3274.
page.heroSubhead: Learn whether you qualify for the VA caregiver stipend — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: If you care for a veteran, the VA may pay you a monthly stipend and provide health coverage and respite. Here is how the program works and who qualifies.

sections.h2: What is the VA caregiver stipend program?
sections.body: The VA caregiver stipend is part of the Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC). It pays a monthly stipend to an approved primary family caregiver of an eligible veteran who needs help with daily activities or supervision due to a serious service-connected injury or illness. Beyond the stipend, approved caregivers may receive CHAMPVA health coverage (if not otherwise insured), caregiver training, mental health services, travel benefits, and respite care. The program is administered through your local VA medical center's Caregiver Support Program.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for the VA caregiver stipend?
sections.body: Eligibility centers on the veteran. Generally, the veteran must have a serious injury or illness connected to their service, need at least six months of in-person personal care, and meet VA disability and care-need criteria. The caregiver must be at least 18 and be the veteran's spouse, family member, or someone who lives with them full time. The VA expanded eligibility in recent years to include veterans from all service eras. Because the criteria are detailed and assessed individually, an official VA eligibility review is the only way to know for certain.

sections.h2: How do I apply for the VA caregiver stipend?
sections.body: Apply online at VA.gov (search 'family caregiver benefits') or call the VA Caregiver Support Line at 855-260-3274. You and the veteran complete a joint application (VA Form 10-10CG), then the VA assesses the veteran's care needs and your caregiving role. A Caregiver Support Coordinator at your local VA medical center guides you through the process. Approval, stipend amount, and benefits depend on the veteran's assessed care level. For help understanding how VA benefits work alongside Medicare and Medigap, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much is the VA caregiver stipend?
faqs.a: The stipend is a monthly payment based on the veteran's assessed level of care and local wage rates, so amounts vary. The VA calculates it individually after approval. Contact the VA Caregiver Support Line at 855-260-3274 for current figures specific to your situation.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for the VA caregiver stipend?
faqs.a: Eligibility requires the veteran to have a serious service-connected injury or illness needing ongoing personal care, and the caregiver to be 18 or older and the veteran's spouse, family member, or full-time household member. The VA assesses each case individually.

faqs.q: How do I apply for the VA family caregiver program?
faqs.a: Apply online at VA.gov or call the VA Caregiver Support Line at 855-260-3274. You and the veteran submit a joint application (VA Form 10-10CG), then the VA assesses care needs. A Caregiver Support Coordinator helps guide you.

faqs.q: Does the VA caregiver program include health insurance?
faqs.a: Yes, for approved primary caregivers who are not already entitled to care or services under another health plan, the program may provide CHAMPVA health coverage, plus mental health services, training, and respite care.

faqs.q: Can I get the VA stipend and Medicare at the same time?
faqs.a: Yes, VA caregiver benefits and the veteran's Medicare can coexist; they serve different purposes. For help understanding how VA, Medicare, and Medigap fit together for your family, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

children.keyword: how to care for aging parent

page.metaTitle: How to Care for an Aging Parent | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: A practical guide to caring for an aging parent — health, finances, safety, and support. Get free senior caregiving help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Care for an Aging Parent
page.quickAnswer: To care for an aging parent, start by assessing their health, safety, finances, and wishes, then build a support team and plan for care needs. Use your Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116) for resources, secure legal documents early, and protect your own health to avoid burnout.
page.heroSubhead: A clear, step-by-step starting point for caring for Mom or Dad — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Becoming your parent's caregiver can feel overwhelming. This guide breaks it into clear first steps so you can act with confidence instead of crisis.

sections.h2: Where do I start when caring for an aging parent?
sections.body: Begin with an honest assessment across four areas: health (conditions, medications, doctors), safety (home hazards, driving, falls), finances and legal (income, bills, power of attorney, advance directives), and daily living (meals, hygiene, transportation, social connection). Talk with your parent about their wishes while they can share them. Then build a team — siblings, doctors, and community resources. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for a needs assessment and connections to meals, transportation, and in-home help. Writing down what you learn turns a vague worry into an actionable plan.

sections.h2: How do I handle health, money, and legal matters?
sections.body: Get organized early. Keep a current medication list and a list of doctors, and consider going to key appointments. Secure legal documents while your parent has capacity: durable power of attorney, healthcare proxy, and advance directives (living will). Review income, Social Security, insurance, and Medicare coverage, and watch for scams targeting seniors. Understanding Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid is often the biggest financial lever — the right supplement can shield your parent from large out-of-pocket bills. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help reviewing their coverage.

sections.h2: How do I care for myself while caring for a parent?
sections.body: You cannot pour from an empty cup. Watch for caregiver burnout — exhaustion, resentment, withdrawing — and build in respite from the start. Share duties with siblings, use adult day programs or in-home aides, and join a caregiver support group through your Area Agency on Aging. Keep your own medical appointments and connections. Accepting help is not weakness; it is what lets you keep caring well over the long term. If stress becomes overwhelming, the 988 Lifeline offers free 24/7 support.

faqs.q: What is the first step in caring for an aging parent?
faqs.a: Start with an honest assessment of your parent's health, home safety, finances, legal documents, and daily living needs, plus a conversation about their wishes. Then contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for a needs assessment and local resources.

faqs.q: What legal documents do I need for an aging parent?
faqs.a: Key documents include a durable power of attorney (finances), a healthcare proxy or medical power of attorney, and advance directives such as a living will. Arrange these while your parent still has the capacity to make decisions.

faqs.q: How do I pay for care for an aging parent?
faqs.a: Funding can come from your parent's income and savings, Medicare and Medigap for medical costs, Medicaid for long-term care, the VA for veterans, and long-term care insurance. Reviewing Medicare and Medigap coverage is often the biggest money-saver.

faqs.q: How do I avoid burnout while caring for a parent?
faqs.a: Build in respite from the start, share duties with family, use adult day programs and in-home aides, join a caregiver support group, and protect your own health and appointments. Watch for early burnout signs like exhaustion and resentment.

faqs.q: Who can help me understand my parent's Medicare coverage?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free, no-pressure help reviewing your parent's Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid options so they are protected from large medical bills. Call 1-800-633-4427.

children.keyword: how to get paid to care for elderly parent

page.metaTitle: Get Paid to Care for Elderly Parent | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how to get paid to care for an elderly parent through Medicaid, the VA, and state programs. See if you qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Get Paid to Care for an Elderly Parent
page.quickAnswer: To get paid to care for an elderly parent, the most common routes are Medicaid self-directed care programs (in most states), the VA caregiver stipend for eligible veterans, long-term care insurance that allows family caregivers, and some state-funded programs. Eligibility depends on your parent's income, state, and care needs.
page.heroSubhead: Find out which programs may pay you to care for Mom or Dad — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: If you are already caring for an elderly parent, you may be able to get paid for it. Here are the real programs that pay family caregivers and how to check your eligibility.

sections.h2: How can I get paid to care for my elderly parent?
sections.body: Several programs pay family caregivers. The most common is Medicaid: most states run self-directed or consumer-directed care programs and Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers that let an eligible senior hire and pay a family member. The VA's Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers pays a monthly stipend to qualifying caregivers of eligible veterans. Some long-term care insurance policies reimburse family caregivers, and a handful of states fund their own caregiver-pay programs. Original Medicare, by contrast, generally does not pay family members for personal care.

sections.h2: Who qualifies, and how much can I earn?
sections.body: Eligibility usually depends on your parent, not you: their income and assets (for Medicaid), their veteran status (for VA programs), or their insurance policy terms. Pay rates vary by program, state, and the level of care your parent needs, and are often set at local home-care wage levels. Some programs restrict whether a spouse or legal guardian can be the paid caregiver. Because rules differ widely from state to state, an official eligibility review is the only way to know your exact options and pay.

sections.h2: How do I apply to get paid as a caregiver?
sections.body: Start by identifying your parent's coverage. For Medicaid, contact your state Medicaid office or Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116) and ask about self-directed care and HCBS waivers. For veterans, call the VA Caregiver Support Line at 855-260-3274. Review any long-term care insurance policy for family-caregiver provisions. The application process can be paperwork-heavy, so get organized and ask for a caregiver support coordinator. For free help understanding how these programs work with Medicare and Medigap, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Can I get paid to take care of my elderly parent?
faqs.a: Often yes. Most states' Medicaid programs allow an eligible senior to hire a family member through self-directed care. The VA pays stipends to caregivers of eligible veterans, and some long-term care policies reimburse family caregivers. Eligibility depends on your parent's situation.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid pay you to take care of a family member?
faqs.a: Yes, in most states. Medicaid self-directed care and HCBS waiver programs let an eligible senior choose and pay a family caregiver. Rules and pay vary by state. Contact your state Medicaid office or Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116.

faqs.q: How much do family caregivers get paid?
faqs.a: Pay varies by program, state, and care level, and is often based on local home-care wage rates. The VA stipend is calculated individually. There is no single national rate, so an official program assessment determines your exact pay.

faqs.q: Can I get paid through Medicare to care for a parent?
faqs.a: Generally no. Original Medicare does not pay family members for personal or custodial care. For caregiver pay, look to Medicaid, the VA, long-term care insurance, or state programs instead.

faqs.q: Who can help me find caregiver-pay programs?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free help understanding how Medicaid, the VA, Medicare, and Medigap work for your family. Call 1-800-633-4427 to explore your options.

page.metaDescription: Recognize the signs of caregiver stress early — anxiety, fatigue, irritability — and find relief. Free senior caregiving help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Signs of caregiver stress include feeling overwhelmed or constantly worried, fatigue, sleep problems, weight or appetite changes, losing interest in activities, irritability, headaches, and using alcohol or food to cope. The Mayo Clinic warns these signs, if ignored, can progress to burnout and serious health problems.
page.heroSubhead: Catch caregiver stress early and get free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Caregiver stress is common, but ignoring it leads to burnout and illness. Here are the signs to watch for and what to do about them.

sections.h2: What are the signs of caregiver stress?
sections.body: According to the Mayo Clinic, common signs of caregiver stress include feeling overwhelmed or constantly worried, feeling tired most of the time, sleeping too much or too little, gaining or losing weight, losing interest in activities you used to enjoy, becoming easily irritated or angry, frequent headaches or body pain, and misusing alcohol, drugs, or food. You may also feel sad, withdraw from friends, or struggle to concentrate. These signs often build gradually, which is why caregivers miss them until they tip into full burnout.

sections.h2: Why caregiver stress matters
sections.body: Caregiver stress is not just uncomfortable — it has real health consequences. Sustained stress weakens the immune system, raises blood pressure, and increases the risk of depression and anxiety. The National Alliance for Caregiving and AARP found that about 1 in 5 caregivers report their own health has declined because of caregiving. When your health suffers, so does the quality of care you provide. Treating your stress is therefore part of caring well for your loved one, not a distraction from it.

sections.h2: What should I do about caregiver stress?
sections.body: Act early. Accept help and delegate tasks, use respite care to take real breaks, and stay connected to friends and a support group. Keep your own medical and dental appointments, move your body, and protect your sleep. Talk to your doctor about your stress, and consider counseling. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 to arrange respite and find local groups. If stress ever feels like a crisis, call or text 988. To find relief programs and benefits that lighten your load, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What are the early signs of caregiver stress?
faqs.a: Early signs include feeling overwhelmed or constantly worried, fatigue, sleep and appetite changes, irritability, losing interest in activities, and frequent headaches. Spotting these early lets you add support before stress becomes full caregiver burnout.

faqs.q: Is caregiver stress the same as burnout?
faqs.a: Not quite. Caregiver stress is the ongoing strain of caregiving; burnout is the deeper physical and emotional exhaustion that results when stress goes unaddressed. Managing stress early helps prevent it from progressing to burnout.

faqs.q: Can caregiver stress make me sick?
faqs.a: Yes. Sustained caregiver stress can weaken your immune system, raise blood pressure, and increase the risk of depression and anxiety. About 1 in 5 caregivers report their health has worsened because of caregiving, per AARP and NAC research.

faqs.q: How do I reduce caregiver stress?
faqs.a: Accept help and delegate, use respite care for real breaks, join a support group, keep your own medical appointments, exercise, and protect your sleep. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for respite and local groups.

faqs.q: Where can I get help managing caregiver stress?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, can connect you to respite, support groups, and benefit programs that ease the load. Call 1-800-633-4427 for free, no-pressure help.

children.keyword: how to deal with caregiver burnout

page.metaTitle: How to Deal With Caregiver Burnout | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Practical steps to deal with caregiver burnout — respite, support groups, and self-care. Get free senior caregiving help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Deal With Caregiver Burnout
page.quickAnswer: To deal with caregiver burnout, share the load: arrange respite care, accept and delegate help, join a caregiver support group, protect your own health and sleep, and set realistic limits. Contact your Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116) for respite; if it feels like a crisis, call or text 988.
page.heroSubhead: Real steps to recover from burnout — plus free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Caregiver burnout is reversible, but not by trying harder. These practical steps help you recover and keep caring for your loved one without losing yourself.

sections.h2: How do I deal with caregiver burnout?
sections.body: The core fix is sharing the load instead of carrying it alone. Start by arranging respite care — in-home aides, adult day programs, or short stays — so you get real breaks. Delegate specific tasks to siblings and friends (give them concrete jobs, not vague offers). Join a caregiver support group to reduce isolation and gather practical tips. Protect your own basics: sleep, meals, movement, and your own medical appointments. Set realistic limits and let go of perfection. Talk to your doctor about your exhaustion, and consider counseling. These steps, used together, are how burnout actually reverses.

sections.h2: How do I get respite care?
sections.body: Respite care is temporary relief that lets you rest or handle your own life. Options include in-home aides, adult day centers, and short-term facility stays. Funding may come from the National Family Caregiver Support Program (no income test), Medicaid HCBS waivers, the VA for veterans' caregivers, or private pay. Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 to learn what is available locally and how to qualify. Even a few hours a week of reliable respite can dramatically lower burnout.

sections.h2: When should I get professional help?
sections.body: Get professional help if exhaustion, hopelessness, or resentment persist, if you are withdrawing from everyone, or if your physical health is slipping. Talk to your own doctor and consider a counselor or therapist experienced with caregivers. If you ever have thoughts of harming yourself or the person you care for, treat it as an emergency and call or text 988, the free 24/7 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. To find respite, support groups, and benefit programs that ease the load, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is the fastest way to relieve caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: The fastest relief comes from real breaks: arrange respite care so you can rest, and delegate specific tasks to others. Even a few reliable hours a week off duty, plus connecting with a support group, can quickly lower burnout.

faqs.q: How do I get respite care for a break?
faqs.a: Contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 to arrange in-home aides, adult day programs, or short-term stays. Funding may come from the National Family Caregiver Support Program, Medicaid waivers, or the VA for veterans' families.

faqs.q: Can caregiver burnout be reversed?
faqs.a: Yes. Burnout is reversible when you share the load through respite, delegation, and support groups, and protect your own sleep, health, and limits. Recovery comes from getting support, not from pushing harder.

faqs.q: When should I see a doctor about caregiver burnout?
faqs.a: See your doctor if exhaustion, hopelessness, or resentment persist, you are withdrawing from everyone, or your physical health is declining. If you have thoughts of self-harm, call or text 988 immediately for free 24/7 crisis support.

faqs.q: Who can help me find relief from burnout?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, connects you to respite, support groups, and benefit programs that lighten the load. Call 1-800-633-4427 for free, no-pressure help.

page.metaDescription: Practical long distance caregiving tips — coordinate care, stay informed, and build a local team from afar. Free senior help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.quickAnswer: Long distance caregiving tips: build a local care team, keep organized records of medications and contacts, use a geriatric care manager or Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116), set up regular check-ins and technology, and plan for emergencies. The National Institute on Aging estimates millions of Americans manage care from afar.
page.heroSubhead: Care well from miles away — plus free senior help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: You can be an effective caregiver even from hundreds of miles away. These tips help you stay informed, coordinated, and ready from a distance.

sections.h2: How can I be an effective long distance caregiver?
sections.body: Distance changes your role from hands-on helper to coordinator and advocate. Build a reliable local team — neighbors, friends, a primary doctor, and ideally a geriatric care manager who can do in-person check-ins. Keep one organized place (digital or paper) with your parent's medications, doctors, insurance, legal documents, and emergency contacts. Set up regular phone or video check-ins so you notice changes early. Contact your parent's Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for local services like meals and transportation. When you visit, use the time to assess safety and meet the care team in person.

sections.h2: What tools and technology help from a distance?
sections.body: Technology shrinks the distance. Video calls let you see how your parent looks and their home environment. Medication reminder systems, medical alert devices with fall detection, and remote monitoring can add safety. Shared digital calendars and care-coordination apps keep siblings and helpers on the same page. Online portals let you access medical records and appointment schedules (with proper authorization). A consumer-friendly notebook of logins, account numbers, and key contacts ensures you can act fast in an emergency, even from far away.

sections.h2: How do I plan for emergencies from afar?
sections.body: Have a plan before you need it. Keep an updated list of your parent's conditions, medications, allergies, doctors, and insurance ready to share with hospitals. Make sure legal documents — healthcare proxy and power of attorney — are in place and that you can access them quickly. Identify a trusted local contact who can respond in minutes. Know your parent's preferred hospital and pharmacy. Understanding their Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid coverage in advance prevents scrambling during a crisis. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help reviewing that coverage so you are ready.

faqs.q: How can I care for a parent who lives far away?
faqs.a: Act as coordinator: build a local care team, keep organized records, schedule regular check-ins, and use a geriatric care manager or Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116) for in-person help. Use visits to assess safety and meet the team.

faqs.q: What is a geriatric care manager?
faqs.a: A geriatric care manager (aging life care professional) is a paid specialist who assesses needs, coordinates services, and provides in-person oversight locally. They are especially valuable for long distance caregivers who cannot be there day to day.

faqs.q: What technology helps long distance caregivers?
faqs.a: Helpful tools include video calls, medical alert devices with fall detection, medication reminders, remote monitoring, shared digital calendars, and care-coordination apps. These help you spot changes early and keep family and helpers coordinated.

faqs.q: How do I prepare for an emergency from far away?
faqs.a: Keep an updated list of conditions, medications, doctors, and insurance; ensure legal documents like a healthcare proxy are accessible; identify a trusted local contact; and know your parent's preferred hospital and pharmacy in advance.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help long distance caregivers?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free help reviewing your parent's Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid coverage and finding local resources, so you are prepared from afar. Call 1-800-633-4427.

page.metaDescription: Learn how caregiver pay from Medicaid works through self-directed care and HCBS waivers. See if you qualify in your state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Caregiver pay from Medicaid is available in most states through self-directed (consumer-directed) care programs and Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, which let an eligible senior hire and pay a family member. Eligibility and pay rates depend on the senior's income and state. Some states limit whether a spouse can be paid.
page.heroSubhead: Find out if Medicaid will pay you to care for a loved one — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Medicaid is the most common way family members get paid to provide care. Here is how it works and how to check whether you qualify in your state.

sections.h2: How does caregiver pay from Medicaid work?
sections.body: Medicaid pays family caregivers mainly through two channels. First, self-directed (or consumer-directed) care programs let an eligible Medicaid recipient manage a budget for their own care and hire a caregiver of their choice — often an adult child or other relative. Second, Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers fund in-home care to help seniors avoid nursing homes, and many allow family members to be paid providers. The senior must qualify for Medicaid (based on income and assets) and meet a care-need level. The caregiver is typically paid at local home-care wage rates.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Medicaid caregiver pay?
sections.body: Qualification depends mostly on the senior. They generally must be enrolled in Medicaid, meet their state's income and asset limits, and need a qualifying level of personal care. Rules vary significantly by state — including whether a spouse or legal guardian can be the paid caregiver, what training is required, and how many hours are covered. Because Medicaid is administered state by state, the same family can qualify in one state and not another. An official eligibility review through your state Medicaid office is the only way to know for sure.

sections.h2: How do I apply for Medicaid caregiver pay?
sections.body: Start by contacting your state Medicaid office or your local Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116, and ask specifically about self-directed care programs and HCBS waivers. If your parent is not yet on Medicaid, you may need to apply and complete a financial and care-needs assessment first; some waivers have waiting lists. Keep documentation organized and ask whether a fiscal intermediary handles payroll and taxes. For free help understanding how Medicaid works alongside Medicare and Medigap, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid pay family caregivers?
faqs.a: Yes, in most states. Through self-directed care programs and HCBS waivers, Medicaid lets an eligible senior hire and pay a family member as their caregiver. Rules and pay rates vary by state, so check with your state Medicaid office.

faqs.q: Can I be paid by Medicaid to care for my spouse or parent?
faqs.a: Often yes for a parent or other relative. Paying a spouse is allowed in some states but restricted in others. Whether you qualify depends on your state's specific Medicaid self-directed care and waiver rules.

faqs.q: How much does Medicaid pay family caregivers?
faqs.a: Pay is usually based on local home-care wage rates and the senior's approved hours of care, so it varies by state and case. There is no single national rate; your state program sets the amount after assessment.

faqs.q: What is an HCBS waiver?
faqs.a: A Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waiver is a Medicaid program that funds in-home and community care to help seniors avoid nursing homes. Many HCBS waivers allow eligible family members to be paid as caregivers.

faqs.q: Who can help me understand Medicaid caregiver pay?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free help understanding how Medicaid, Medicare, and Medigap fit together and how to pursue caregiver pay. Call 1-800-633-4427.

children.keyword: how to care for a parent with dementia

page.metaTitle: How to Care for a Parent With Dementia | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Practical guidance for caring for a parent with dementia — safety, communication, and support. Free senior help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Care for a Parent With Dementia
page.quickAnswer: To care for a parent with dementia, build a safe routine, simplify communication, manage behaviors with patience, secure the home, and plan for increasing care needs. Lean on the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 helpline (800-272-3900) and respite care. Over 6 million Americans live with Alzheimer's, per the Alzheimer's Association.
page.heroSubhead: Compassionate, practical dementia caregiving help — plus free senior support at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Caring for a parent with dementia is uniquely demanding. These practical strategies help you keep them safe, communicate better, and protect your own well-being.

sections.h2: How do I care for a parent with dementia day to day?
sections.body: Structure and patience are everything. Build a predictable daily routine for meals, hygiene, and rest to reduce confusion and anxiety. Simplify communication: speak slowly, use short sentences, ask one question at a time, and offer simple choices rather than open-ended questions. Reduce clutter and noise, and use labels or visual cues around the home. Expect that abilities will change over time, and adjust tasks accordingly. When difficult behaviors arise, look for the cause — pain, hunger, fatigue, or overstimulation — rather than arguing. The Alzheimer's Association 24/7 helpline (800-272-3900) offers free guidance any time.

sections.h2: How do I keep a parent with dementia safe?
sections.body: Safety needs grow as dementia progresses. Remove tripping hazards, install grab bars, and secure or lock away medications, cleaning products, firearms, and car keys when driving is no longer safe. Address wandering with door alarms, secure locks, and an ID bracelet or enrollment in a wandering-response program. Set water heaters to a safe temperature and consider stove safety devices. Plan for sundowning (increased evening confusion) with calm routines and good lighting. Reassess the home regularly, because what was safe last year may not be safe now.

sections.h2: How do I get support and plan ahead?
sections.body: You cannot do this alone, and you should not try. Join a dementia caregiver support group through the Alzheimer's Association or your Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116), and use respite care to take real breaks — dementia caregiving has some of the highest burnout rates. Plan early for legal and financial matters (power of attorney, advance directives) while your parent can still participate, and think ahead about future care levels, including memory care. Understanding Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid coverage helps you prepare for long-term costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help reviewing those options.

faqs.q: How do I communicate with a parent who has dementia?
faqs.a: Speak slowly with short, simple sentences, ask one question at a time, and offer simple choices instead of open-ended questions. Stay calm, avoid arguing or correcting, and use a reassuring tone. Visual cues and a quiet setting help them focus.

faqs.q: How do I handle difficult dementia behaviors?
faqs.a: Look for the underlying cause — pain, hunger, fatigue, fear, or overstimulation — rather than reacting to the behavior itself. Keep routines predictable, reduce noise and clutter, and stay calm. The Alzheimer's Association helpline (800-272-3900) offers free 24/7 guidance.

faqs.q: How do I keep a parent with dementia safe at home?
faqs.a: Remove tripping hazards, install grab bars, secure medications and chemicals, address wandering with door alarms and an ID bracelet, and reassess driving and stove safety. Re-evaluate the home regularly as needs change.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover dementia care?
faqs.a: Medicare covers doctor-ordered medical care, diagnosis, and some skilled services, but generally not long-term custodial care like supervision or help with daily activities. Medicaid, the VA, and long-term care insurance may help with those costs.

faqs.q: Where can I get help caring for a parent with dementia?
faqs.a: Call the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 helpline at 800-272-3900 for caregiving guidance and support groups. For coverage and benefit questions, call 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, at 1-800-633-4427.

children.keyword: how to convince elderly parent to accept help

page.metaTitle: Convince Elderly Parent to Accept Help | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: How to convince an elderly parent to accept help — start early, listen, and preserve their dignity. Free senior caregiving help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP today.
page.h1: How to Convince an Elderly Parent to Accept Help
page.quickAnswer: To convince an elderly parent to accept help, start the conversation early and calmly, listen to their fears about losing independence, involve them in decisions, start small, and frame help as preserving their independence — not taking it away. Enlist a trusted doctor or third party when resistance is strong.
page.heroSubhead: Compassionate ways to help a resistant parent say yes — plus free senior support at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: When an aging parent refuses help, it usually comes from fear of losing independence — not stubbornness. These approaches help you move forward with respect and less conflict.

sections.h2: Why do elderly parents resist help?
sections.body: Resistance almost always has a reason. Many older adults fear losing independence, control, and dignity, or worry that accepting help means moving out of their home. Some are in denial about declining abilities, embarrassed about needing assistance, or anxious about cost. A few have cognitive changes that affect judgment. Understanding the why behind the refusal lets you address the real concern instead of arguing about the surface issue. When your parent feels heard rather than managed, they are far more likely to cooperate.

sections.h2: How do I talk to my parent about accepting help?
sections.body: Start early, before a crisis, and choose a calm, private moment. Listen more than you lecture — ask what worries them and what they want. Involve them as a decision-maker, offering choices rather than ultimatums ("Would you prefer help with meals or housekeeping first?"). Start small with one low-stakes form of help to build trust, then expand. Frame assistance as a way to stay independent and in their home longer, which is usually what they want most. Avoid shaming or taking over, which deepens resistance.

sections.h2: What if my parent still refuses help?
sections.body: Persistent refusal is common, so be patient and revisit the conversation over time. Enlist allies your parent trusts — a doctor, clergy member, friend, or another relative — since the same message often lands better from a third party. Pick your battles, focusing first on safety issues like falls, medication errors, or unsafe driving. If you suspect cognitive decline is driving unsafe choices, ask their physician for an evaluation. Throughout, preserve their dignity. For help finding services and understanding coverage that makes accepting help easier, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Why won't my elderly parent accept help?
faqs.a: Most often, fear of losing independence, control, and dignity, plus worry about moving out of their home or about cost. Some are in denial or embarrassed. Understanding the real fear behind the refusal helps you address it directly.

faqs.q: How do I start the conversation about accepting help?
faqs.a: Start early, before a crisis, in a calm and private moment. Listen to their concerns, involve them as a decision-maker with choices rather than ultimatums, and frame help as a way to stay independent and in their home longer.

faqs.q: What if my parent keeps refusing help?
faqs.a: Be patient and revisit it over time. Enlist trusted allies like their doctor or clergy, since the message often lands better from a third party. Prioritize safety issues first, and ask a physician to evaluate if cognitive decline seems involved.

faqs.q: Should I force my parent to accept help?
faqs.a: Avoid forcing or taking over, which usually deepens resistance, unless there is a serious safety emergency. Focus on preserving dignity, addressing real fears, and starting small. For unsafe situations involving cognition, involve their physician.

faqs.q: Who can help me find services my parent will accept?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, can help you find in-home services, respite, and benefit programs, and explain coverage that makes accepting help easier. Call 1-800-633-4427 for free.

children.keyword: what to do when aging parents need help

page.metaTitle: What to Do When Aging Parents Need Help | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: What to do when aging parents need help — assess needs, build a plan, and find resources fast. Free senior caregiving help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: What to Do When Aging Parents Need Help
page.quickAnswer: When aging parents need help, first assess their health, safety, finances, and wishes, then contact your Area Agency on Aging (800-677-1116) for a needs assessment and local resources. Secure legal documents, build a care team, review Medicare and Medicaid coverage, and protect your own health to avoid burnout.
page.heroSubhead: A calm action plan for when Mom or Dad needs help — plus free senior support at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.intro: Realizing your aging parents need help can be stressful and sudden. Here is a clear, prioritized plan so you can act with confidence instead of panic.

sections.h2: What should I do first when aging parents need help?
sections.body: Start with a calm assessment, not a crisis reaction. Look at four areas: health (conditions, medications, recent changes), safety (falls, driving, home hazards), finances and legal (bills, income, power of attorney, advance directives), and daily living (meals, hygiene, isolation). Talk with your parents about what they want while they can tell you. Then contact your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for a professional needs assessment and connections to meals, transportation, and in-home help. Writing down what you find turns overwhelm into a clear, prioritized to-do list.

sections.h2: How do I build a care plan and team?
sections.body: No one should manage aging parents alone. Divide responsibilities among siblings and family by strengths and availability, and write down who does what. Bring in professionals as needed — doctors, in-home aides, a geriatric care manager, or an elder law attorney for legal documents. Tap community resources through your Area Agency on Aging and 2-1-1. Review your parents' Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid coverage early, because the right plan can prevent large out-of-pocket bills and unlock services. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help reviewing that coverage.

sections.h2: How do I take care of myself in the process?
sections.body: Helping aging parents is a marathon, so protect yourself from the start. Watch for caregiver stress and burnout — exhaustion, resentment, withdrawing — and build in respite early. Share the load, use adult day programs and in-home help, and join a caregiver support group through your Area Agency on Aging. Keep your own medical appointments, sleep, and relationships. Accepting help is what lets you keep showing up for your parents over the long haul. If stress ever feels like a crisis, the 988 Lifeline offers free 24/7 support.

faqs.q: What is the first thing to do when aging parents need help?
faqs.a: Start with a calm assessment of their health, safety, finances, legal documents, and daily living needs, and ask what they want. Then call your Area Agency on Aging at 800-677-1116 for a needs assessment and local resources.

faqs.q: Who do I call when my elderly parents need help?
faqs.a: Call the Eldercare Locator at 800-677-1116 to reach your local Area Agency on Aging for assessments and services, dial 2-1-1 for local social services, and call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help with coverage and resources.

faqs.q: How do I get my siblings to help with aging parents?
faqs.a: Divide responsibilities by each person's strengths and availability, and write down who does what to prevent confusion and resentment. Hold regular check-ins, and consider a family meeting or a geriatric care manager to mediate if needed.

faqs.q: How do I pay for help when aging parents need care?
faqs.a: Funding can come from your parents' income, Medicare and Medigap for medical costs, Medicaid for long-term care, the VA for veterans, and long-term care insurance. Reviewing Medicare and Medigap coverage early often saves the most money.

faqs.q: Where can I get free help figuring out next steps?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for all things senior, offers free, no-pressure help finding resources and reviewing your parents' Medicare, Medigap, and Medicaid coverage. Call 1-800-633-4427.
